title: Working Like a Lawyer with Claude
summary: Mark Pike from Anthropic and Anna Gressel from Freshfields discuss how lawyers use Claude for legal work. They explore how AI is changing legal practice while maintaining that final judgment calls remain with human lawyers.
writeup: A conversation between an Anthropic product lawyer and Freshfields' global co-head of AI on how enterprise legal teams are deploying Claude in practice. The central argument: chat-based Q&A is table stakes, but the real unlock is using Claude to synthesize large bodies of documents (memos, legislation, roadmaps) into new artifacts via code. The through-line is the tension between AI's speed and scale and the irreplaceable judgment a law firm provides when navigating regulatory risk.
key_points: Claude Code's ability to create Word documents, Excel spreadsheets, and PowerPoint files from a knowledge worker's local computer represents a qualitative leap beyond chat-based Q&A for legal professionals.

Anna's core use case: uploading a large, heterogeneous corpus (existing memos, product roadmaps, new legislation) and having Claude synthesize across all of it to produce a new artifact, a task where AI's ability to spot themes at scale is particularly strong.

Freshfields operates with roughly 5,700 staff and lawyers worldwide who are using AI, and the 'context gap' (understanding what the model spec allows today versus tomorrow as engineering moves fast) is consistently the stickiest legal navigation challenge.
Junior associates often carry the sharpest intuitive grasp of both AI's benefits and its limitations, including the right process for checking outputs and staying in the loop.
The firm frames its enduring value as judgment: when a client is navigating a rocky strait, the lawyer's role is keeping the rudder straight, and that judgment cannot be delegated to the model.
Anthropic's internal value of 'holding light and shade' maps directly onto how lawyers advise clients: acknowledging serious risks in AI technology while also recognizing its substantial promise.

AI and the Future of Legal Work
